{{Short description|Form of racial prejudice}}
{{Multiple issues|
{{Globalize|date=April 2021}}
{{Essay|date=June 2024}}
{{Bias|date=June 2025}}
}}

In [[psychology]] and [[sociology]], '''adultification''' is a form of treatment of some [[Child|children]] by [[Adult|adults]] as being more mature than they actually are—such as when they are expected to take on age-inappropriate adult roles and responsibilities as children, and when they are taken to be as culpable as adults for any crime or wrongdoing. 

In the [[United States]], it is often used to refer to a form of [[Racism|racial bias]], whereby black American children are assumed to be more culpable, and punished more harshly than white children, for the same offences.<ref name="Dancy-2014">{{cite journal | doi=10.1080/10665684.2014.959271 | title=(Un)Doing Hegemony in Education: Disrupting School-to-Prison Pipelines for Black Males | date=2014 | last1=Dancy | first1=T. Elon | journal=Equity & Excellence in Education | volume=47 | issue=4 | pages=476–493 | s2cid=145679452 }}</ref><ref name="Goff-2014">{{cite journal | doi=10.1037/a0035663 | title=The essence of innocence: Consequences of dehumanizing Black children | date=2014 | last1=Goff | first1=Phillip Atiba | last2=Jackson | first2=Matthew Christian | last3=Di Leone | first3=Brooke Allison Lewis | last4=Culotta | first4=Carmen Marie | last5=Ditomasso | first5=Natalie Ann | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume=106 | issue=4 | pages=526–545 | pmid=24564373 }}</ref>
Many studies have found that Black children are more likely to receive discipline from authority figures, such as police officers and teachers.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Epstein">{{cite web|last1=Epstein |first1=Rebecca |last2=Blake |first2=Jamilia J. |last3=Gonzalez |first3=Thalia |url=https://genderjusticeandopportunity.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/girlhood-interrupted.pdf |title=Girlhood Interrupted: The Erasure of Black Girls' Childhood |publisher=Georgetown Law Center on Poverty and Inequality |access-date=2022-06-13}}</ref><ref name="Reynolds-2016">{{cite journal |jstor=10.5323/blachistbull.79.2.0012 |last1=Reynolds |first1=Aja |last2=Hicks |first2=Stephanie D. |title=Can We Live? Working Toward a Praxis of Support for Carefree Black Girls |journal=Black History Bulletin |date=2016 |volume=79 |issue=2 |pages=12–17 |doi=10.1353/bhb.2016.0000 |s2cid=245666004 }}</ref><ref name="Morris-2007">{{cite journal | doi=10.1177/0044118X06296778 | title="Ladies" or "Loudies"? | date=2007 | last1=Morris | first1=Edward W. | journal=Youth & Society | volume=38 | issue=4 | pages=490–515 | s2cid=143916502 }}</ref><ref name="Dumas-2016">{{cite journal | doi=10.17763/0017-8055.86.1.27 | title=(Re)Imagining Black Boyhood: Toward a Critical Framework for Educational Research | date=2016 | last1=Dumas | first1=Michael J. | last2=Nelson | first2=Joseph Derrick | journal=Harvard Educational Review | volume=86 | pages=27–47 }}</ref><ref name="CarterAndrews-2019">{{cite journal | doi=10.3102/0002831219849392 | title=The Impossibility of Being "Perfect and White": Black Girls' Racialized and Gendered Schooling Experiences | date=2019 | last1=Carter Andrews | first1=Dorinda J. | last2=Brown | first2=Tashal | last3=Castro | first3=Eliana | last4=Id-Deen | first4=Effat | journal=American Educational Research Journal | volume=56 | issue=6 | pages=2531–2572 | s2cid=181408681 }}</ref> Black children are also overlooked or their intentions are misrepresented in healthcare settings, contributing to "medical mistrust" in the Black community.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hoffman |first1=Kelly |last2=Trawalter |first2=Sophie |last3=Axt |first3=Jordan |last4=Norman Oliver |first4=M. |title=Racial bias in pain assessment and treatment recommendations, and false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites |journal= Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|date=2016 |volume=113 |issue=16 |pages=4296–4301 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1516047113 |pmid=27044069 |pmc=4843483 |bibcode=2016PNAS..113.4296H |doi-access=free }}</ref> Scholars from the ''[[Georgetown Law Center on Poverty and Law]]'' have argued that adultification bias can trace its roots to slavery and [[stereotypes of African Americans]].<ref name="Epstein" /><ref name="Morris-2007" /><ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021">{{cite journal | doi=10.1353/fem.2021.0000 | title=Masked Violence against Black Women and Girls | date=2021 | last1=Smith-Purviance | first1=Ashley L. | journal=Feminist Studies | volume=47 | pages=175–200 }}</ref> Adultification bias can affect the language used when describing children or adolescences of minority groups in the media.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /> This bias may perpetuate the [[school-to-prison pipeline]].<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Reynolds-2016" /><ref name="Hines-2021">{{cite journal |last1=Hines |first1=Erik |last2=Fletcher |first2=Edward |last3=Ford |first3=Donna |last4=Moore |first4=James |title=Preserving Innocence: Ending Perceived Adultification and Toxic Masculinity Toward Black Boys |journal=Journal of Family Strengths |date=10 December 2021 |volume=21 |issue=1 |doi=10.58464/2168-670X.1444 |url=https://digitalcommons.library.tmc.edu/jfs/vol21/iss1/1/ |doi-access=free }}</ref>

[[Teacher|Educators]] and authority figures can address adultification bias by improving their [[cultural competence]] and [[communication]].<ref name="Guevara-2006">{{cite journal | doi=10.1177/1557085106292778 | title=Gender and Juvenile Justice Decision Making | date=2006 | last1=Guevara | first1=Lori | last2=Herz | first2=Denise | last3=Spohn | first3=Cassia | journal=Feminist Criminology | volume=1 | issue=4 | pages=258–282 | s2cid=146347745 }}</ref><ref name="Koch-2022">{{cite journal | doi=10.1177/15248399211049207 | title=Addressing Adultification of Black Pediatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Framework to Decrease Disparities | date=2022 | last1=Koch | first1=Amie | last2=Kozhumam | first2=Arthi | journal=Health Promotion Practice | volume=23 | issue=4 | pages=555–559 | pmid=34693783 | s2cid=239766430 }}</ref>

== History ==
''Adultification'' was originally a [[psychology]] term describing children who act more mature than their peers as a result of being handed adult responsibilities from a young age.<ref name="Epstein" /> This adultification is often forced by circumstances outside of the Black youth's control. Maturity usually comes with increased autonomy, Black children are frequently denied this right while still facing the expectations of others.

=== Slavery in the United States ===
Many Black children were treated like adults during [[slavery]].<ref name="Teshome-2018">{{cite journal | doi=10.1215/07990537-7249292 | title=Not Child but Meager | date=2018 | last1=Teshome | first1=Tezeru | last2=Yang | first2=K. Wayne | journal=Small Axe: A Caribbean Journal of Criticism | volume=22 | issue=3 | pages=160–170 | s2cid=149534656 }}</ref><ref name="Hines-2021" /> Treatment of enslaved children varied throughout the United States. Some plantations would not assign jobs to children until they became adolescents.<ref name="Pargas-2011">{{cite journal | doi=10.1080/0144039X.2011.601618 | title=From the Cradle to the Fields: Slave Childcare and Childhood in the Antebellum South | date=2011 | last1=Pargas | first1=Damian Alan | journal=Slavery & Abolition | volume=32 | issue=4 | pages=477–493 | s2cid=143877395 }}</ref> Others put Black children to work as early as 2 to 3-years-old.<ref name="Epstein" /><ref name="Dumas-2016" /><ref name="Pargas-2011" /> Slave owners would then decide when enslaved children made the transition from doing light chores to working hard-labor jobs assigned to adults.<ref name="Pargas-2011" /> Typically, this transition occurred by the time they were eight to nine.<ref name="Pargas-2011" /><ref>United States Work Projects Administration. ''Slave Narratives: A Folk History of Slavery in the United States from Interviews with Former Slaves. Administrative Files Records Bearing on the History of the Slave Narratives''. 2004-10-25. ''Project Gutenberg'', https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/13847.</ref> Adultification also occurred due to [[Hypersexualization|hyper-sexualization]] of Black children by slave owners.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Loft |first=Margaret |title=Syrup, Stereotypes, and Sexualization: A Historical Analysis of the Hyper-Sexualization of the Black Female Body and the Predominating Stereotypes of Black Women |url=https://pilotscholars.up.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1030&context=hst_studpubs |journal=University of Portland History Program}}</ref>

=== Media representation ===
Although White adolescence is frequently pictured through films, Black adolescence is not commonly portrayed.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Shannon |first=Linita |date=2008 |title=Deconstructing the Black Coming-of-Age Film and its Effect on the Negotiation of Black Identity |url=https://surface.syr.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1557&context=honors_capstone |journal=Syracuse University Honors Program}}</ref><ref name="Rodgers-2022">{{Cite journal |last=Rodgers |first=Jimmonique R. S. |date=2022-06-11 |title=The Rage of Innocence: How America Criminalizes Black Youth |journal=Crime Prevention and Community Safety |volume=24 |issue=3 |pages=304–306 |doi=10.1057/s41300-022-00151-3 |issn=1460-3780 |s2cid=256506635}}</ref> This lack of media representation contributes to the ignorance surrounding Black youth and their struggles with forced adultification. The aging of Black youth prevents them from having the same privileges as White youth.<ref name="Rodgers-2022" />

The journalist Arwa Mahdawi has identified adultification by [[CNN]] and [[Sky News]] in the coverage of deaths of children in the [[Gaza war]].<ref name="e775">{{cite news| last=Mahdawi | first=Arwa | title=The adultification of Palestinian children has consequences and needs to stop | work=The Guardian | date=4 May 2024 | url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/article/2024/may/04/adultification-children-palestine-us | access-date=14 April 2025}}</ref>

== Consequences ==

=== Education ===
Black students may face hypercriticism from authority figures, especially while in school.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Goff-2014" /> Teachers can perceive Black girls as being loud or "sassy" compared to white girls and punish them over being "unladylike".<ref name="Reynolds-2016" /><ref name="Morris-2007" /> Teachers are less likely to help Black students when they need help in school since they are assumed to be delinquents and incapable of success.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" /> Because of their perceived maturity, Black children are seen as less in need of access to [[mentorship]] and leadership opportunities.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Epstein" /> Black children are treated to "know better" by teachers and are not seen as having one of the main characteristics of childhood: innocence.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Goff-2014" /><ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" /> Black students may also be penalized more over mistakes on schoolwork.<ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" />

Black girls are more likely to be disciplined in school than white girls for subjective infractions including, but not limited to dress code violations, fighting, [[loitering]], and harassment.<ref name="Epstein" /> They are more susceptible to verbal and physical violence by authority figures in schools. The [[United States Department of Education]] found that Black girls are six times more likely to be suspended than white girls.<ref name="Reynolds-2016" /> Not only are Black girls found to have high rate of discipline in school over white girls, they tend to have higher rates than boys belonging to other racial/ethnic groups, further highlighting this bias in Black students.<ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" />

Some schools, however, do have Black and other minority boys consistently being disciplined over other groups of children.<ref name="Morris-2007" /> Black boys are three times more likely than other students to be suspended from school.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /> Overall, black boys have the highest suspension and drop out rates from both elementary and high school.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /> According to the [[National Center for Education Statistics]], the largest group with out-of-school suspensions from public schools is black boys, accounting for 17.6 percent of suspensions from 2013 to 2014. From that same study, they found that Black girls account for the second largest percentage of out-of-school suspensions at 9.6 percent.<ref>de Brey, C., Musu, L., McFarland, J., Wilkinson-Flicker, S., Diliberti, M., Zhang, A., Branstetter, C., and Wang, X. (2019). Status and Trends in the Education of Racial and Ethnic Groups 2018 (NCES 2019-038). U.S. Department of Education. Washington, DC: National Center for Education Statistics. Retrieved 2022-06-14 from https://nces.ed.gov/pubsearch/.</ref>

During the 1980s and 1990s, more schools started to rely on policies like drug-sniffing dogs and armed police officers in response to student misbehavior.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /> Some say that these policies may actually exacerbate anti-Blackness through school policies such as these because they create environments in which Black girls learn their feelings do not matter and that they will not receive the benefit of the doubt over misbehavior.<ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" /> Some of the students can tell when these teachers treat Black students differently from white students and are left isolated from students and teachers because of this treatment.<ref name="Goff-2014" /><ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" /> When Black children speak up over the bias, they are seen as "talking back" or being too assertive rather than expressing their concerns and usually receive some type of disciplinary action.<ref name="Morris-2007" /><ref name="CarterAndrews-2019" /><ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" /> Witnessing the harsh discipline of these students in schools can desensitize others to situations when Black students are given worse punishments.<ref name="Smith-Purviance-2021" />

=== Justice system ===
Between 1985 and 1997, the number of Black girls in the juvenile justice system was growing at a rate higher than any other population.<ref name="Reynolds-2016" /> Black girls are 2.7 times more likely to be referred to juvenile justice over white girls, and young Black girls are 1.2 times more likely to be detained by the police.<ref name="Epstein" /><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1080/07418825.2010.532146 | title=Are Girls Getting Tougher, or Are We Tougher on Girls? Probability of Arrest and Juvenile Court Oversight in 1980 and 2000 | date=2011 | last1=Stevens | first1=Tia | last2=Morash | first2=Merry | last3=Chesney-Lind | first3=Meda | journal=Justice Quarterly | volume=28 | issue=5 | pages=719–744 | s2cid=145506297 }}</ref>

Adultification bias may contribute to the school-to-prison pipeline by having Black children punished in schools and increasing their chances of being placed in prison or wrongfully imprisoned.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /><ref name="Hines-2021" /> Students that have been suspended are three times more likely to drop out of school and those who drop out are three times more likely to be incarcerated.<ref name="Dancy-2014" /> Black youth are three times more likely than White youth to be put in residential placement.<ref name="Guevara-2006" /> A study showed that participants viewed Black juveniles as more similar to adults than to White juveniles when blamed for a crime.<ref name="Rattan-2012">{{cite journal | doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0036680 | doi-access=free | title=Race and the Fragility of the Legal Distinction between Juveniles and Adults | date=2012 | last1=Rattan | first1=Aneeta | last2=Levine | first2=Cynthia S. | last3=Dweck | first3=Carol S. | last4=Eberhardt | first4=Jennifer L. | journal=PLOS ONE | volume=7 | issue=5 | article-number=e36680 | pmid=22649496 | pmc=3359323 | bibcode=2012PLoSO...736680R }}</ref> This can impact teens being detained as being treated like adults, despite the precedent that shows that juveniles are less culpable than adult offenders.<ref name="Epstein" /><ref name="Rattan-2012" />

=== Healthcare ===
Black teens are often tested for sexually transmitted diseases when presenting with abdominal symptoms despite reporting no sexual activity due to the assumption of Black promiscuity.<ref name="Koch-2022" /><ref name="Johnson-2013">{{cite journal |last1=Johnson |first1=Tiffani J. |last2=Weaver |first2=Matthew D. |last3=Borrero |first3=Sonya |last4=Davis |first4=Esa M. |author-link4=Esa Matius Davis |last5=Myaskovsky |first5=Larissa |last6=Zuckerbraun |first6=Noel S. |last7=Kraemer |first7=Kevin L. |date=2013 |title=Association of Race and Ethnicity with Management of Abdominal Pain in the Emergency Department |journal=Pediatrics |volume=132 |issue=4 |pages=e851–e858 |doi=10.1542/peds.2012-3127 |pmc=4074647 |pmid=24062370}}</ref> A 2020 study found that Black youth were less likely to be promptly diagnosed and treated for [[appendicitis]], revealing implicit and structural racial bias in hospitals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Goyal |first=Monika K. |date=2021 |title=Racial and ethnic disparities in the delayed diagnosis of appendicitis among children |journal=Academic Emergency Medicine |volume=28 |issue=9 |pages=949–956 |doi=10.1111/acem.14142 |pmid=32991770 |s2cid=222167151 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Academic study ==
A 2017 study on Black girls by the [[Georgetown Center on Gender Justice & Opportunity]] includes one of the first studies of "adultification bias", showing that adults perceive Black girls as more adult-like than their white peers. That study followed a 2014 study by Philip Goff on Black boys, showing that Black boys are viewed as older and more culpable for crimes than white boys of the same age.{{Fact|date=December 2024}}

== In Brazil ==
In [[Brazil]], the phenomenon also manifests itself in the form of racial bias and early hypersexualization embedded in the broader context of [[structural racism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Querências afetivas: interconexões on/offline de mulheres cisgêneras e travestis negras|url=http://repositorio.ufc.br/handle/riufc/79067|date=2023|language=pt-BR|first=Jéssica Silva de|last=Sousa|journal=Federal University of Ceará}}</ref> Data from the "''Violence against women''" platform indicate that, between 2011 and 2017, more than 45% of sexual abuse cases registered in the country involved black girls aged 0 to 9, a percentage more than 7% higher than that observed among white girls in the same period.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=A ESCOLA E A EROTIZAÇÃO DOS CORPOS INFANTIS NEGROS|url=https://doi.org/10.18616/rsp.v7i1.8145|journal=Revista Saberes Pedagógicos|date=2023-07-21|issn=2526-4559|pages=136–155|volume=7|issue=1|doi=10.18616/rsp.v7i1.8145|first=Andressa Klissia Oliveira|last=Carvalho|first2=Giani|last2=Rabelo|doi-access=free}}</ref>{{unclear inline|reason=7% higher in what measure? Absolute? Relative? Per capita? And when we say "involved", do we mean to include perpetrators?|date=March 2026}}

In 2021, Brazilian influencer [[Felipe Neto]] warned about a trend on [[TikTok]] where children dance to erotic lyrics,<ref>{{Cite web |last=redacaoterra |title=Felipe Neto faz denúncia sobre sexualização infantil |url=https://www.terra.com.br/diversao/gente/felipe-neto-faz-denuncia-sobre-sexualizacao-infantil,abc0edf60a1b90fa6d344a022c2a69af16bnddyo.html |access-date=February 3, 2026 |website=[[Terra (company)|Terra]] |language=pt-BR}}</ref> in 2019, he accused YouTube of facilitating pedophiles.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Felipe Neto critica YouTube por não tomar atitude contra pedófilos |url=https://bahia.ba/entretenimento/felipe-neto-critica-ao-youtube-por-nao-tomar-atitude-contra-pedofilos/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250814143855/https://bahia.ba/entretenimento/felipe-neto-critica-ao-youtube-por-nao-tomar-atitude-contra-pedofilos/ |archive-date=August 14, 2025 |access-date=February 3, 2026 |work=Bahia.Ba |language=pt-br}}</ref> In 2024, actress and presenter {{Interlanguage link|Antônia Fontenelle|pt}} published a video denouncing influencer [[Hytalo Santos]], who gained notoriety for producing videos in a format similar to reality shows, filmed in houses or mansions where children and teenagers shared their routines, for exploitation and sexualization of minors, and was the subject of a court decision that ordered the immediate removal of the content published by her and imposed a daily fine in case of non-compliance.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Grande |first=Antigo Campo |date=2025-08-12 |title=Antônia Fontenelle denunciou influenciador por sexualização de menores; Justiça determinou apenas a remoção do vídeo |url=https://acgnews.com.br/antonia-fontenelle-denunciou-influenciador-por-sexualizacao-de-menores-justica-determinou-apenas-a-remocao-do-video/174566/ |access-date=February 3, 2026 |website=ACG News |language=pt-BR}}</ref>

==In the United Kingdom==

Adultification bias has been raised as an issue in the treatment of Black children in the UK.<ref name="w226">{{cite news| last=Mohdin | first=Aamna | title='They saw me as calculating, not a child': how adultification leads to black children being treated as criminals | work=The Guardian | date=5 July 2022 | url=https://www.theguardian.com/society/2022/jul/05/they-saw-me-as-calculating-not-a-child-how-adultification-leads-to-black-children-being-treated-as-criminals | access-date=14 April 2025}}</ref> It has been described as a "systemic" issue, and has been a factor in cases where children have been treated as criminals, or where sexual abuse of children has not been recognised.<ref name="w226"/> In 2024, the [[Independent Office for Police Conduct]] issued guidelines intending to prevent it, but [[Black Lives Matter]] UK said that the guidelines would not make enough difference.<ref name="j672">{{cite news| last=Mohdin | first=Aamna | title=Watchdog calls for end to 'adultification' of black children by police in England and Wales | work=The Guardian | date=28 November 2024 | url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2024/nov/28/adultification-black-children-police-england-wales-iopc-watchdog | access-date=14 April 2025}}</ref>
